agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 bounded contexts mapped to 6 deployable services, prioritizing deployment independence between the highest-change-rate domains (Orders, Payments, Location Tracking). The decomposition follows domain boundaries identified via DDD strategic design, uses asynchronous domain events as the primary integration mechanism, and keeps the team's moderate operational maturity as a hard constraint — meaning we start with 6 services max, not 12. A shared API Gateway fronts external traffic, and an event bus (e.g., Kafka or managed equivalent) decouples cross-service communication.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle: placement, status transitions, cancellation. Owns the Order aggregate including line items and delivery address. Emits OrderPlaced, OrderCancelled, OrderDelivered events.","boundaries":"Owns orders table/schema. Does NOT know courier identity or payment details — communicates via events and synchronous calls only at well-defined API boundaries."},{"name":"Dispatch Service","responsibility":"Courier assignment, route optimization, and dispatch decisions. Consumes OrderPlaced events, runs assignment algorithm, emits CourierAssigned. Owns courier availability state and active assignment records.","boundaries":"Owns courier-assignment and routing data. Reads courier location from Location Service via internal API. Does not own courier profiles (that stays in Courier/Account context)."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests and stores real-time courier GPS updates (5K writes/sec peak). Exposes current-position queries and short-term trajectory. Publishes LocationUpdated events for consumers that need coarse-grained position changes (e.g., customer-facing ETA).","boundaries":"Owns location time-series data. Optimized for high-throughput writes (append-only store). Stateless ingestion tier scales horizontally. No business logic beyond geo-validation."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Processes charges, holds, refunds, and payouts. Enforces strict transactional consistency for financial operations. Integrates with external payment gateway (Stripe/Adyen). Emits PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued events.","boundaries":"Owns payment records, ledger entries, and refund state. Isolated database with ACID guarantees. No other service writes to payment tables. Idempotency keys on all mutations."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Delivers customer-facing notifications across email, SMS, and push channels. Consumes domain events (OrderPlaced, CourierAssigned, OrderDelivered, etc.) and applies notification rules/templates.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates, delivery logs, and channel preferences. Generic — no domain logic. Treats all upstream events as triggers."},{"name":"Customer Account Service","responsibility":"Customer registration, authentication, profile management, and courier profile management. Issues auth tokens consumed by API Gateway for request authentication.","boundaries":"Owns customer and courier profile data, credentials, preferences. Auth boundary — all other services trust tokens issued here."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Order Service","contract":"REST — POST /orders, GET /orders/{id}, PATCH /orders/{id}/cancel","coupling_type":"runtime"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous REST — POST /payments/authorize (called during order placement to hold funds). Timeout + retry with idempotency key.","coupling_type":"runtime (synchronous for consistency)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes OrderPlaced, OrderCancelled, OrderDelivered events (CloudEvents envelope, JSON payload)","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"Event Bus","to":"Dispatch Service","contract":"Consumes OrderPlaced → triggers courier assignment","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"Dispatch Service","to":"Location Service","contract":"Synchronous REST — GET /couriers/{id}/location (read current position for assignment algorithm)","coupling_type":"runtime (read-only, cacheable)"},{"from":"Dispatch Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes CourierAssigned, CourierEnRoute, DeliveryCompleted","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"Location Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes LocationUpdated (throttled to ~1/min per courier for downstream consumers; raw 10s updates stay internal)","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"Event Bus","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Consumes all customer-relevant events, maps to notification templates","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"Event Bus","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Consumes OrderDelivered → captures held payment; Consumes OrderCancelled → releases hold","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Customer Account Service","contract":"REST — auth endpoints, profile CRUD. Gateway validates JWT on every inbound request.","coupling_type":"runtime"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer places order → API Gateway → Order Service validates, calls Payment Service synchronously to authorize funds, persists order, emits OrderPlaced. 2. Dispatch Service consumes OrderPlaced, queries Location Service for nearby courier positions, runs assignment, emits CourierAssigned. 3. Courier app sends GPS pings every 10s → API Gateway → Location Service (high-throughput write path). Location Service publishes throttled LocationUpdated for ETA consumers. 4. On delivery confirmation, Dispatch Service emits DeliveryCompleted → Order Service transitions status, emits OrderDelivered → Payment Service captures funds, Notification Service sends receipt. 5. All customer-facing state changes trigger Notification Service via events.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Database-per-Service","rationale":"Enforces bounded context data ownership. Critical for Payment Service isolation (ACID) and Location Service optimization (time-series store). Prevents deployment coupling through shared schemas."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Choreography)","rationale":"Decouples services temporally. 8-person team cannot afford a centralized orchestrator service to maintain. Choreography with well-defined events keeps each service autonomous. Use a dead-letter queue + retry for reliability."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Team should NOT do a big-bang rewrite. Extract one service at a time starting with Location Service (highest write load, most distinct data model, least business logic coupling), then Payment, then remaining services."},{"pattern":"API Gateway","rationale":"Single entry point for external clients (customer app, courier app). Handles auth token validation, rate limiting, and request routing. Avoids clients needing to know service topology."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Payment Service requires idempotency keys on all write operations to handle retries safely. Network partitions between Order and Payment services make this non-negotiable."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Location Service only)","rationale":"Location Service separates the high-throughput write path (GPS ingestion) from the read path (current position queries). Write path uses an append-only time-series store; read path serves latest-position from a materialized view or cache."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId, contains LineItems, DeliveryAddress, Status)","OrderStatus (value object: Placed → Assigned → InTransit → Delivered → Cancelled)"]},{"name":"Dispatch & Routing","classification":"core","aggregates":["Assignment (root: AssignmentId, links OrderId ↔ CourierId, tracks assignment lifecycle)","CourierAvailability (root: CourierId, tracks on/off duty, current capacity)"]},{"name":"Location Tracking","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierLocation (root: CourierId, current Position value object, timestamp)","LocationHistory (append-only, no aggregate — event stream)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: TransactionId, links to OrderId, Amount, Status: Authorized → Captured → Refunded)","Ledger (append-only record of all financial events for auditability)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: NotificationId, Channel, Template, Recipient, DeliveryStatus)"]},{"name":"Customer Identity","classification":"generic","aggregates":["CustomerAccount (root: CustomerId, profile, auth credentials, preferences)","CourierProfile (root: CourierId, profile, vehicle info, documents)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Dispatch & Routing","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Order Management publishes OrderPlaced; Dispatch consumes. Order team accommodates Dispatch's data needs in the event schema.)"},{"upstream":"Dispatch & Routing","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Dispatch publishes DeliveryCompleted; Order Management updates status.)"},{"upstream":"Location Tracking","downstream":"Dispatch & Routing","relationship":"Open Host Service (Location exposes a query API; Dispatch is one of several consumers.)"},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Order lifecycle events drive payment capture/release.)"},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist (Notifications conform to upstream event schemas without negotiation.)"},{"upstream":"Dispatch & Routing","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist"},{"upstream":"Customer Identity","downstream":"All Services","relationship":"Open Host Service (Auth tokens are a published language consumed by all services via API Gateway.)"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Synchronous call from Order Service → Payment Service for fund authorization during order placement","gains":"Strong consistency — customer gets immediate feedback on payment failure. No need for compensating transactions on the happy path.","sacrifices":"Runtime coupling between Order and Payment. If Payment Service is down, order placement fails.","alternatives_considered":["Async payment authorization with eventual consistency (rejected: poor UX — customer waits without confirmation)","Saga with reservation pattern (rejected: over-engineering for this team size, adds orchestration complexity)"]},{"decision":"6 services, not more granular decomposition","gains":"Manageable for an 8-person team. Each pair of engineers can own 1-2 services. Reduces operational overhead (fewer deployments, fewer network hops, fewer things to monitor).","sacrifices":"Some services (e.g., Dispatch) combine assignment and routing — may need to split later if routing algorithm complexity grows independently.","alternatives_considered":["Separate Routing Service from Dispatch (rejected: premature — same team, same release cadence, same data today)","Merge Notification into other services (rejected: notification channel logic is generic and benefits from isolation)"]},{"decision":"Event choreography over orchestration","gains":"No central orchestrator to maintain. Each service is autonomous. Lower coupling. Simpler to reason about individual services.","sacrifices":"Harder to trace end-to-end flows. Requires investment in distributed tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) and dead-letter queue monitoring.","alternatives_considered":["Central saga orchestrator (rejected: additional service to build and operate, bottleneck, team lacks experience)"]},{"decision":"Strangler Fig migration starting with Location Service","gains":"Location Service has the most distinct performance profile (time-series writes) and least business logic coupling. Low-risk first extraction that builds team confidence and operational muscle.","sacrifices":"Full benefits of decomposition not realized until later services are extracted. Intermediate state has monolith + 1 service, which is awkward.","alternatives_considered":["Start with Payment Service (rejected: highest consistency requirements make it a risky first extraction)","Big-bang rewrite (rejected: 3 years of monolith logic, 8-person team — too risky)"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"New channels (WhatsApp, in-app) added by implementing a new channel adapter in Notification Service. No other service changes. Template system allows business team to modify notification content without deploys."},{"area":"Dispatch algorithm","evolution_path":"Assignment algorithm can evolve from simple nearest-courier to ML-based optimization. Contained within Dispatch Service. Interface (consume OrderPlaced, query Location, emit CourierAssigned) stays stable."},{"area":"Location Service data store","evolution_path":"Can swap from PostgreSQL+TimescaleDB to a dedicated time-series database (InfluxDB, QuestDB) or Redis for hot path without affecting any other service — data store is behind the service API boundary."},{"area":"Payment provider","evolution_path":"Payment Service wraps external provider behind an Anti-Corruption Layer. Switching from Stripe to Adyen is an internal change to Payment Service only."},{"area":"Event bus technology","evolution_path":"Start with managed Kafka (or even Redis Streams for lower operational burden). Can migrate to Kafka/Pulsar later. Services publish/consume via a thin client library that abstracts the broker."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Event schemas","reason":"Once published, event schemas become contracts. Breaking changes require versioning (e.g., OrderPlaced.v2) and a deprecation window. Invest in schema registry (Avro/Protobuf with compatibility checks) early."},{"area":"Order ↔ Payment synchronous coupling","reason":"This is a deliberate rigidity point. The synchronous call during order placement is load-bearing for UX consistency. Changing this to async requires rethinking the customer-facing order placement flow."},{"area":"API Gateway as single entry point","reason":"All external traffic routes through the gateway. Gateway configuration (routing rules, rate limits) must be updated when services change their API surface. This is manageable but becomes a coordination point."},{"area":"Courier ID as cross-context identifier","reason":"CourierId is referenced in Dispatch, Location, and Customer Identity contexts. It is a shared identifier (not a shared entity). Any change to ID format affects multiple services."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Team operational maturity gap — moving from 1 deployable to 6 introduces distributed systems failure modes (network partitions, partial failures, data inconsistency) the team has not operated before.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Strangler Fig migration: extract one service at a time over 3-6 months. Invest in observability (distributed tracing, centralized logging, alerting) BEFORE extracting the first service. Run game days to practice failure scenarios."},{"risk":"Data consistency across services — order status, payment status, and dispatch status must stay coherent. Events can be lost or delayed.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use a transactional outbox pattern to guarantee event publication. Dead-letter queues with alerting for failed consumers. Implement idempotent event handlers. Build a reconciliation job that detects drift between Order and Payment states."},{"risk":"Location Service write throughput (5K writes/sec) requires careful infrastructure choices. Wrong data store or architecture leads to data loss or latency spikes.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use append-only time-series storage (TimescaleDB or similar). Stateless ingestion tier behind a load balancer. Batch writes where possible. Load test to 2x peak (10K/sec) before go-live."},{"risk":"Event schema evolution breaks consumers silently.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Schema registry with backward compatibility enforcement. Consumer-driven contract tests. Never remove or rename fields — only add. Version events when breaking changes are unavoidable."},{"risk":"Distributed tracing gaps make debugging cross-service issues painful.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Mandate correlation IDs (trace-id) on all requests and events from day one. Use OpenTelemetry. This is a prerequisite, not a nice-to-have."},{"risk":"Premature extraction — splitting too many services before the team has learned to operate even one.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Hard rule: do not extract service N+1 until service N has been stable in production for at least 4 weeks. The Strangler Fig pace is set by operational confidence, not a project timeline."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-4): Establish foundations — set up event bus infrastructure, API Gateway, distributed tracing, and CI/CD pipeline for multi-service deployment. No service extraction yet. Phase 2 (Weeks 5-12): Extract Location Service using Strangler Fig. Route GPS write traffic to new service while monolith still handles reads; then migrate reads. Phase 3 (Weeks 13-20): Extract Payment Service with transactional outbox. Requires careful data migration and dual-write period. Phase 4 (Weeks 21-30): Extract Notification Service (low risk, event-driven), then Dispatch Service, then Order Service, then Customer Account Service. The monolith shrinks with each extraction until it is an empty shell.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-microservice-decomposition.md — Architecture Decision Record capturing this design","docs/adr/002-event-bus-selection.md — ADR for event bus technology choice","docs/adr/003-location-service-extraction.md — Detailed plan for first extraction","services/location-service/ — New service skeleton (API, ingestion, store)","services/payment-service/ — Extracted after Location Service stabilizes","infrastructure/api-gateway/ — Gateway configuration (Kong/Envoy/cloud-native)","infrastructure/event-bus/ — Event bus setup and topic definitions","shared/event-schemas/ — Schema definitions (Avro/Protobuf) with versioning","shared/observability/ — OpenTelemetry configuration, correlation ID middleware"],"patterns_to_follow":"Transactional Outbox for reliable event publishing. Database-per-service with no shared schemas. API Gateway for external traffic. Internal service-to-service calls use REST with circuit breakers and timeouts. Events use CloudEvents envelope format. All services expose health checks and metrics endpoints. Feature flags to control traffic routing during Strangler Fig migration.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT share databases between services — this recreates monolith coupling at the data layer","Do NOT build a distributed monolith — if every request requires synchronous calls to 4 services, you have worse than a monolith","Do NOT skip the observability investment — you will be flying blind in production","Do NOT extract all services simultaneously — the team will be overwhelmed operationally","Do NOT use synchronous calls where eventual consistency is acceptable — prefer events","Do NOT ignore event ordering — use partition keys (e.g., OrderId, CourierId) to guarantee per-entity ordering in Kafka","Do NOT let the 'shared library' grow into a coupling vector — keep it thin (event schemas, auth middleware only)"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary identification — these follow clear domain boundaries with distinct data ownership and change cadences","Location Service as first extraction target — most distinct performance profile, least business logic coupling","Event-driven integration as primary pattern — well-suited to the team size and domain"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact throughput handling for Location Service — 5K writes/sec is achievable with many stacks but the right choice depends on existing team expertise (PostgreSQL+TimescaleDB vs. dedicated TSDB vs. Redis)","Migration timeline estimates — highly dependent on monolith code quality and test coverage, which are unknown","Whether Dispatch and Routing should be one service or two — made a pragmatic call to merge them, but this could be wrong if routing complexity is already high"],"assumptions":["The monolith uses a single relational database (likely PostgreSQL)","The team has CI/CD in place but limited experience with container orchestration","Event bus infrastructure does not exist yet and must be built","The 8 engineers can dedicate meaningful time to the migration alongside feature work","Current monolith has reasonable test coverage to validate behavior preservation during extraction"]}}

The decomposition follows domain boundaries identified via DDD strategic design, uses asynchronous domain events as the primary integration mechanism, and keeps the team's moderate operational maturity as a hard constraint — meaning we start with 6 services max, no

The decomposition follows domain boundaries identified via DDD strategic design, uses asynchronous domain events as the primary integration mechanism, and keeps the team's moderate operational maturity as a hard constraint — meaning we start with 6 services max, not 12. A shared API Gateway fronts external traffic, and an event bus (e.g., Kafka or managed equivalent) decouples cross-service communication.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle: placement, status transitions, cancellation. Owns the Order aggregate including line items and delivery address. Emits OrderPlaced, OrderCancelled, OrderDelivered events.","boundaries":"Owns orders table/schema. Does NOT know courier identity or payment details — communicates via events and synchronous calls only at well-defined API boundaries."},{"name":"Dispatch Service","responsibility":"Courier assignment, route optimization, and dispatch decisions. Consumes OrderPlaced events, runs assignment algorithm, emits CourierAssigned. Owns courier availability state and active assignment records.","boundaries":"Owns courier-assignment and routing data. Reads courier location from Location Service via internal API. Does not own courier profiles (that stays in Courier/Account context)."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests and stores real-time courier GPS updates (5K writes/sec peak). Exposes current-position queries and short-term trajectory. Publishes LocationUpdated events for consumers that need coarse-grained position changes (e.g., customer-facing ETA).","boundaries":"Owns location time-series data. Optimized for high-throughput writes (append-only store). Stateless ingestion tier scales horizontally. No business logic beyond geo-validation."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Processes charges, holds, refunds, and payouts. Enforces strict transactional consistency for financial operations. Integrates with external payment gateway (Stripe/Adyen). Emits PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued events.","boundaries":"Owns payment records, ledger entries, and refund state. Isolated database with ACID guarantees. No other service writes to payment tables. Idempotency keys on all mutations."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Delivers customer-facing notifications across email, SMS, and push channels. Consumes domain events (OrderPlaced, CourierAssigned, OrderDelivered, etc.) and applies notification rules/templates.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates, delivery logs, and channel preferences. Generic — no domain logic. Treats all upstream events as triggers."},{"name":"Customer Account Service","responsibility":"Customer registration, authentication, profile management, and courier profile management. Issues auth tokens consumed by API Gateway for request authentication.","boundaries":"Owns customer and courier profile data, credentials, preferences. Auth boundary — all other services trust tokens issued here."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Order Service","contract":"REST — POST /orders, GET /orders/{id}, PATCH /orders/{id}/cancel","coupling_type":"runtime"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous REST — POST /payments/authorize (called during order placement to hold funds). Gateway validates JWT on every inbound request.","coupling_type":"runtime"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer places order → API Gateway → Order Service validates, calls Payment Service synchronously to authorize funds, persists order, emits OrderPlaced. 2. Dispatch Service consumes OrderPlaced, queries Location Service for nearby courier positions, runs assignment, emits CourierAssigned. 3. Courier app sends GPS pings every 10s → API Gateway → Location Service (high-throughput write path). Location Service publishes throttled LocationUpdated for ETA consumers. 4. Use a dead-letter queue + retry for reliability."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Team should NOT do a big-bang rewrite. Build a reconciliation job that detects drift between Order and Payment states."},{"risk":"Location Service write throughput (5K writes/sec) requires careful infrastructure choices. Wrong data store or architecture leads to data loss or latency spikes.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use append-only time-series storage (TimescaleDB or similar). Stateless ingestion tier behind a load balancer. Batch writes where possible. Load test to 2x peak (10K/sec) before go-live."},{"risk":"Event schema evolution breaks consumers silently.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Schema registry with backward compatibility enforcement. Consumer-driven contract tests. Never remove or rename fields — only add. Version events when breaking changes are unavoidable."},{"risk":"Distributed tracing gaps make debugging cross-service issues painful.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Mandate correlation IDs (trace-id) on all requests and events from day one. Use OpenTelemetry. This is a prerequisite, not a nice-to-have."},{"risk":"Premature extraction — splitting too many services before the team has learned to operate even one.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Hard rule: do not extract service N+1 until service N has been stable in production for at least 4 weeks. The Strangler Fig pace is set by operational confidence, not a project timeline."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-4): Establish foundations — set up event bus infrastructure, API Gateway, distributed tracing, and CI/CD pipeline for multi-service deployment. No service extraction yet. Phase 2 (Weeks 5-12): Extract Location Service using Strangler Fig. Route GPS write traffic to new service while monolith still handles reads; then migrate reads. Phase 3 (Weeks 13-20): Extract Payment Service with transactional outbox. Requires careful data migration and dual-write period. Phase 4 (Weeks 21-30): Extract Notification Service (low risk, event-driven), then Dispatch Service, then Order Service, then Customer Account Service. The monolith shrinks with each extraction until it is an empty shell.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-microservice-decomposition.md — Architecture Decision Record capturing this design","docs/adr/002-event-bus-selection.md — ADR for event bus technology choice","docs/adr/003-location-service-extraction.md — Detailed plan for first extraction","services/location-service/ — New service skeleton (API, ingestion, store)","services/payment-service/ — Extracted after Location Service stabilizes","infrastructure/api-gateway/ — Gateway configuration (Kong/Envoy/cloud-native)","infrastructure/event-bus/ — Event bus setup and topic definitions","shared/event-schemas/ — Schema definitions (Avro/Protobuf) with versioning","shared/observability/ — OpenTelemetry configuration, correlation ID middleware"],"patterns_to_follow":"Transactional Outbox for reliable event publishing. Database-per-service with no shared schemas. API Gateway for external traffic. Internal service-to-service calls use REST with circuit breakers and timeouts. Events use CloudEvents envelope format. All services expose health checks and metrics endpoints. Feature flags to control traffic routing during Strangler Fig migration.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT share databases between services — this recreates monolith coupling at the data layer","Do NOT build a distributed monolith — if every request requires synchronous calls to 4 services, you have worse than a monolith","Do NOT skip the observability investment — you will be flying blind in production","Do NOT extract all services simultaneously — the team will be overwhelmed operationally","Do NOT use synchronous calls where eventual consistency is acceptable — prefer events","Do NOT ignore event ordering — use partition keys (e.g., OrderId, CourierId) to guarantee per-entity ordering in Kafka","Do NOT let the 'shared library' grow into a coupling vector — keep it thin (event schemas, auth middleware only)"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary identification — these follow clear domain boundaries with distinct data ownership and change cadences","Location Service as first extraction target — most distinct performance profile, least business logic coupling","Event-driven integration as primary pattern — well-suited to the team size and domain"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact throughput handling for Location Service — 5K writes/sec is achievable with many stacks but the right choice depends on existing team expertise (PostgreSQL+TimescaleDB vs. dedicated TSDB vs. Redis)","Migration timeline estimates — highly dependent on monolith code quality and test coverage, which are unknown","Whether Dispatch and Routing should be one service or two — made a pragmatic call to merge them, but this could be wrong if routing complexity is already high"],"assumptions":["The monolith uses a single relational database (likely PostgreSQL)","The team has CI/CD in place but limited experience with container orchestration","Event bus infrastructure does not exist yet and must be built","The 8 engineers can dedicate meaningful time to the migration alongside feature work","Current monolith has reasonable test coverage to validate behavior preservation during extraction"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 bounded contexts mapped to 6 deployable services, prioritizing deployment independence between the highest-change-rate domains (Orders, Payments, Location Tracking). The decomposition follows domain boundaries identified via DDD strategic design, uses asynchronous domain events as the primary integration mechanism, and keeps the team's moderate operational maturity as a hard constraint — meaning we start with 6 services max, not 12. A shared API Gateway fronts external traffic, and an event bus (e.g., Kafka or managed equivalent) decouples cross-service communication.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle: placement, status transitions, cancellation. Owns the Order aggregate including line items and delivery address. Emits OrderPlaced, OrderCancelled, OrderDelivered events.","boundaries":"Owns orders table/schema. Does NOT know courier identity or payment details — communicates via events and synchronous calls only at well-defined API boundaries."},{"name":"Dispatch Service","responsibility":"Courier assignment, route optimization, and dispatch decisions. Consumes OrderPlaced events, runs assignment algorithm, emits CourierAssigned. Owns courier availability state and active assignment records.","boundaries":"Owns courier-assignment and routing data. Reads courier location from Location Service via internal API. Does not own courier profiles (that stays in Courier/Account context)."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests and stores real-time courier GPS updates (5K writes/sec peak). Exposes current-position queries and short-term trajectory. Publishes LocationUpdated events for consumers that need coarse-grained position changes (e.g., customer-facing ETA).","boundaries":"Owns location time-series data. Optimized for high-throughput writes (append-only store). Stateless ingestion tier scales horizontally. No business logic beyond geo-validation."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Processes charges, holds, refunds, and payouts. Enforces strict transactional consistency for financial operations. Integrates with external payment gateway (Stripe/Adyen). Emits PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued events.","boundaries":"Owns payment records, ledger entries, and refund state. Isolated database with ACID guarantees. No other service writes to payment tables. Idempotency keys on all mutations."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Delivers customer-facing notifications across email, SMS, and push channels. Consumes domain events (OrderPlaced, CourierAssigned, OrderDelivered, etc.) and applies notification rules/templates.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates, delivery logs, and channel preferences. Generic — no domain logic. Treats all upstream events as triggers."},{"name":"Customer Account Service","responsibility":"Customer registration, authentication, profile management, and courier profile management. Issues auth tokens consumed by API Gateway for request authentication.","boundaries":"Owns customer and courier profile data, credentials, preferences. Auth boundary — all other services trust tokens issued here."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Order Service","contract":"REST — POST /orders, GET /orders/{id}, PATCH /orders/{id}/cancel","coupling_type":"runtime"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous REST — POST /payments/authorize (called during order placement to hold funds). Timeout + retry with idempotency key.","coupling_type":"runtime (synchronous for consistency)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes OrderPlaced, OrderCancelled, OrderDelivered events (CloudEvents envelope, JSON payload)","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"Event Bus","to":"Dispatch Service","contract":"Consumes OrderPlaced → triggers courier assignment","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"Dispatch Service","to":"Location Service","contract":"Synchronous REST — GET /couriers/{id}/location (read current position for assignment algorithm)","coupling_type":"runtime (read-only, cacheable)"},{"from":"Dispatch Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes CourierAssigned, CourierEnRoute, DeliveryCompleted","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"Location Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes LocationUpdated (throttled to ~1/min per courier for downstream consumers; raw 10s updates stay internal)","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"Event Bus","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Consumes all customer-relevant events, maps to notification templates","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"Event Bus","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Consumes OrderDelivered → captures held payment; Consumes OrderCancelled → releases hold","coupling_type":"async event"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Customer Account Service","contract":"REST — auth endpoints, profile CRUD. Gateway validates JWT on every inbound request.","coupling_type":"runtime"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer places order → API Gateway → Order Service validates, calls Payment Service synchronously to authorize funds, persists order, emits OrderPlaced. 2. Dispatch Service consumes OrderPlaced, queries Location Service for nearby courier positions, runs assignment, emits CourierAssigned. 3. Courier app sends GPS pings every 10s → API Gateway → Location Service (high-throughput write path). Location Service publishes throttled LocationUpdated for ETA consumers. 4. On delivery confirmation, Dispatch Service emits DeliveryCompleted → Order Service transitions status, emits OrderDelivered → Payment Service captures funds, Notification Service sends receipt. 5. All customer-facing state changes trigger Notification Service via events.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Database-per-Service","rationale":"Enforces bounded context data ownership. Critical for Payment Service isolation (ACID) and Location Service optimization (time-series store). Prevents deployment coupling through shared schemas."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Choreography)","rationale":"Decouples services temporally. 8-person team cannot afford a centralized orchestrator service to maintain. Choreography with well-defined events keeps each service autonomous. Use a dead-letter queue + retry for reliability."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Team should NOT do a big-bang rewrite. Extract one service at a time starting with Location Service (highest write load, most distinct data model, least business logic coupling), then Payment, then remaining services."},{"pattern":"API Gateway","rationale":"Single entry point for external clients (customer app, courier app). Handles auth token validation, rate limiting, and request routing. Avoids clients needing to know service topology."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Payment Service requires idempotency keys on all write operations to handle retries safely. Network partitions between Order and Payment services make this non-negotiable."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Location Service only)","rationale":"Location Service separates the high-throughput write path (GPS ingestion) from the read path (current position queries). Write path uses an append-only time-series store; read path serves latest-position from a materialized view or cache."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId, contains LineItems, DeliveryAddress, Status)","OrderStatus (value object: Placed → Assigned → InTransit → Delivered → Cancelled)"]},{"name":"Dispatch & Routing","classification":"core","aggregates":["Assignment (root: AssignmentId, links OrderId ↔ CourierId, tracks assignment lifecycle)","CourierAvailability (root: CourierId, tracks on/off duty, current capacity)"]},{"name":"Location Tracking","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierLocation (root: CourierId, current Position value object, timestamp)","LocationHistory (append-only, no aggregate — event stream)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: TransactionId, links to OrderId, Amount, Status: Authorized → Captured → Refunded)","Ledger (append-only record of all financial events for auditability)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: NotificationId, Channel, Template, Recipient, DeliveryStatus)"]},{"name":"Customer Identity","classification":"generic","aggregates":["CustomerAccount (root: CustomerId, profile, auth credentials, preferences)","CourierProfile (root: CourierId, profile, vehicle info, documents)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Dispatch & Routing","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Order Management publishes OrderPlaced; Dispatch consumes. Order team accommodates Dispatch's data needs in the event schema.)"},{"upstream":"Dispatch & Routing","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Dispatch publishes DeliveryCompleted; Order Management updates status.)"},{"upstream":"Location Tracking","downstream":"Dispatch & Routing","relationship":"Open Host Service (Location exposes a query API; Dispatch is one of several consumers.)"},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Order lifecycle events drive payment capture/release.)"},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist (Notifications conform to upstream event schemas without negotiation.)"},{"upstream":"Dispatch & Routing","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist"},{"upstream":"Customer Identity","downstream":"All Services","relationship":"Open Host Service (Auth tokens are a published language consumed by all services via API Gateway.)"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Synchronous call from Order Service → Payment Service for fund authorization during order placement","gains":"Strong consistency — customer gets immediate feedback on payment failure. No need for compensating transactions on the happy path.","sacrifices":"Runtime coupling between Order and Payment. If Payment Service is down, order placement fails.","alternatives_considered":["Async payment authorization with eventual consistency (rejected: poor UX — customer waits without confirmation)","Saga with reservation pattern (rejected: over-engineering for this team size, adds orchestration complexity)"]},{"decision":"6 services, not more granular decomposition","gains":"Manageable for an 8-person team. Each pair of engineers can own 1-2 services. Reduces operational overhead (fewer deployments, fewer network hops, fewer things to monitor).","sacrifices":"Some services (e.g., Dispatch) combine assignment and routing — may need to split later if routing algorithm complexity grows independently.","alternatives_considered":["Separate Routing Service from Dispatch (rejected: premature — same team, same release cadence, same data today)","Merge Notification into other services (rejected: notification channel logic is generic and benefits from isolation)"]},{"decision":"Event choreography over orchestration","gains":"No central orchestrator to maintain. Each service is autonomous. Lower coupling. Simpler to reason about individual services.","sacrifices":"Harder to trace end-to-end flows. Requires investment in distributed tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) and dead-letter queue monitoring.","alternatives_considered":["Central saga orchestrator (rejected: additional service to build and operate, bottleneck, team lacks experience)"]},{"decision":"Strangler Fig migration starting with Location Service","gains":"Location Service has the most distinct performance profile (time-series writes) and least business logic coupling. Low-risk first extraction that builds team confidence and operational muscle.","sacrifices":"Full benefits of decomposition not realized until later services are extracted. Intermediate state has monolith + 1 service, which is awkward.","alternatives_considered":["Start with Payment Service (rejected: highest consistency requirements make it a risky first extraction)","Big-bang rewrite (rejected: 3 years of monolith logic, 8-person team — too risky)"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"New channels (WhatsApp, in-app) added by implementing a new channel adapter in Notification Service. No other service changes. Template system allows business team to modify notification content without deploys."},{"area":"Dispatch algorithm","evolution_path":"Assignment algorithm can evolve from simple nearest-courier to ML-based optimization. Contained within Dispatch Service. Interface (consume OrderPlaced, query Location, emit CourierAssigned) stays stable."},{"area":"Location Service data store","evolution_path":"Can swap from PostgreSQL+TimescaleDB to a dedicated time-series database (InfluxDB, QuestDB) or Redis for hot path without affecting any other service — data store is behind the service API boundary."},{"area":"Payment provider","evolution_path":"Payment Service wraps external provider behind an Anti-Corruption Layer. Switching from Stripe to Adyen is an internal change to Payment Service only."},{"area":"Event bus technology","evolution_path":"Start with managed Kafka (or even Redis Streams for lower operational burden). Can migrate to Kafka/Pulsar later. Services publish/consume via a thin client library that abstracts the broker."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Event schemas","reason":"Once published, event schemas become contracts. Breaking changes require versioning (e.g., OrderPlaced.v2) and a deprecation window. Invest in schema registry (Avro/Protobuf with compatibility checks) early."},{"area":"Order ↔ Payment synchronous coupling","reason":"This is a deliberate rigidity point. The synchronous call during order placement is load-bearing for UX consistency. Changing this to async requires rethinking the customer-facing order placement flow."},{"area":"API Gateway as single entry point","reason":"All external traffic routes through the gateway. Gateway configuration (routing rules, rate limits) must be updated when services change their API surface. This is manageable but becomes a coordination point."},{"area":"Courier ID as cross-context identifier","reason":"CourierId is referenced in Dispatch, Location, and Customer Identity contexts. It is a shared identifier (not a shared entity). Any change to ID format affects multiple services."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Team operational maturity gap — moving from 1 deployable to 6 introduces distributed systems failure modes (network partitions, partial failures, data inconsistency) the team has not operated before.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Strangler Fig migration: extract one service at a time over 3-6 months. Invest in observability (distributed tracing, centralized logging, alerting) BEFORE extracting the first service. Run game days to practice failure scenarios."},{"risk":"Data consistency across services — order status, payment status, and dispatch status must stay coherent. Events can be lost or delayed.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use a transactional outbox pattern to guarantee event publication. Dead-letter queues with alerting for failed consumers. Implement idempotent event handlers. Build a reconciliation job that detects drift between Order and Payment states."},{"risk":"Location Service write throughput (5K writes/sec) requires careful infrastructure choices. Wrong data store or architecture leads to data loss or latency spikes.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use append-only time-series storage (TimescaleDB or similar). Stateless ingestion tier behind a load balancer. Batch writes where possible. Load test to 2x peak (10K/sec) before go-live."},{"risk":"Event schema evolution breaks consumers silently.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Schema registry with backward compatibility enforcement. Consumer-driven contract tests. Never remove or rename fields — only add. Version events when breaking changes are unavoidable."},{"risk":"Distributed tracing gaps make debugging cross-service issues painful.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Mandate correlation IDs (trace-id) on all requests and events from day one. Use OpenTelemetry. This is a prerequisite, not a nice-to-have."},{"risk":"Premature extraction — splitting too many services before the team has learned to operate even one.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Hard rule: do not extract service N+1 until service N has been stable in production for at least 4 weeks. The Strangler Fig pace is set by operational confidence, not a project timeline."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-4): Establish foundations — set up event bus infrastructure, API Gateway, distributed tracing, and CI/CD pipeline for multi-service deployment. No service extraction yet. Phase 2 (Weeks 5-12): Extract Location Service using Strangler Fig. Route GPS write traffic to new service while monolith still handles reads; then migrate reads. Phase 3 (Weeks 13-20): Extract Payment Service with transactional outbox. Requires careful data migration and dual-write period. Phase 4 (Weeks 21-30): Extract Notification Service (low risk, event-driven), then Dispatch Service, then Order Service, then Customer Account Service. The monolith shrinks with each extraction until it is an empty shell.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-microservice-decomposition.md — Architecture Decision Record capturing this design","docs/adr/002-event-bus-selection.md — ADR for event bus technology choice","docs/adr/003-location-service-extraction.md — Detailed plan for first extraction","services/location-service/ — New service skeleton (API, ingestion, store)","services/payment-service/ — Extracted after Location Service stabilizes","infrastructure/api-gateway/ — Gateway configuration (Kong/Envoy/cloud-native)","infrastructure/event-bus/ — Event bus setup and topic definitions","shared/event-schemas/ — Schema definitions (Avro/Protobuf) with versioning","shared/observability/ — OpenTelemetry configuration, correlation ID middleware"],"patterns_to_follow":"Transactional Outbox for reliable event publishing. Database-per-service with no shared schemas. API Gateway for external traffic. Internal service-to-service calls use REST with circuit breakers and timeouts. Events use CloudEvents envelope format. All services expose health checks and metrics endpoints. Feature flags to control traffic routing during Strangler Fig migration.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT share databases between services — this recreates monolith coupling at the data layer","Do NOT build a distributed monolith — if every request requires synchronous calls to 4 services, you have worse than a monolith","Do NOT skip the observability investment — you will be flying blind in
